name: Swedish Tacos
recipeIngredient: 2 to 4 cloves of garlic, minced coarsely
2 medium Yellow onions or 1 huge one, chopped into pieces about a quarter to half an inch square
1 large Block of cheddar cheese, grated on the side of the grater with big holes
2 to 3 tomatoes, cut into chunks about the same size as the onions or slightly larger
Head of iceberg lettuce, shredded finely into strips maybe 1/8 inch wide
recipeInstructions:
I've decided to share at long last the only two recipes I can call my own. Both these were developed before I really knew how to cook, in times of poverty (which seems to come and go with alarming regularity). They're good and cheap, an unbeatable combination.
Here's number one... Copious quantities of ice cold beer is the best accompaniment for this. This dish is a truly international one and bears little resemblance to anything from either Sweden (I'm part Swedish) or Mexico. The inspiration is Mexican, the utensils and some of the techniques used are Chinese, the ingredients are all-American (with the exception of the tortillas). It's "peasant food" in the best tradition - cheap, full of flavor, healthy and satisfying.
I cooked these so often that I got to where I could knock out a complete, filling dinner even with the frozen hamburger in less than half an hour. Follow the recipe closely at least the first time to see what it's supposed to taste like. I've never had anyone dislike this recipe, with the possible exception of a vegetarian or two.
Slice and dice all ingredients first. Put everything into separate serving dishes as you cut them.
Heat a wok over high heat and add about 2 tablespoons of peanut oil. Let it heat for a minute then toss in the minced garlic, followed a second or two later by about 2/3rds of the chopped yellow onion. The onions and garlic should start smelling good in the kitchen.
Reduce the heat to medium and stir the onions and garlic until they are well browned. Remove the cooked onions with a slotted spoon or spatula and set aside.
Take the frozen hamburger out of its wrapping and add it to the wok as is. Push the onions and garlic out of the way so the surface of the frozen hamburger is in contact with the hot surface of the wok. If you haven't already, now is the time to chop or mince the remaining chilies.
As the hamburger browns, scrape off the browned surface with a slotted spoon or spatula. Flip the block of meat over and scrape off the browned bits. Let the other side brown slightly then repeat the process until both sides are well browned. Browning the onions and garlic gives a nice sharp, distinctive savoriness to the finished dish.
Salt the meat periodically while it's cooking. The reason for using frozen hamburger is that when it's all finally cooked, you'll have bits of meat ranging from well done and crunchy to nearly raw. This gives an interesting texture and deepens the range of tastes. Lower the heat under the wok toward the end if needed to avoid overcooking the meat.
Assemble the tacos by spreading a layer of meat (not too thick), hot chilies, cheddar cheese, chopped raw onions, tomatoes, and lettuce on each tortilla. Anoint generously with Tabasco sauce or Jamaican Pickapeppa sauce before folding.
The finished dish is an incredible medley of tastes and textures, each distinct and flavorful, with at least two kinds of heat. Great food that you eat with your fingers.
